petitioner_background: The petitioner is a research scientist in the sciences field, specifically working in areas including electrophoresis, electrokinetic microfluidic systems, liver cancer detection devices, and biofuels production. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. His career includes postdoctoral research, employment at a company (apparently Caliper or similar), authorship of seven scientific articles and a handbook chapter, and two patent applications (one briefly licensed).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a travel grant from GRC (Gordon Research Conference, redacted in text) issued to help finance his attendance at a conference in August 2003. The grant was subtracted from his registration fee and was issued based on one of the petitioner's unidentified poster abstracts. The petitioner characterized this travel grant as an award.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director did not address this criterion. The AAO found the petitioner submitted no evidence to establish that GRC issues travel grants based on excellence in the field of endeavor. Additionally, the AAO noted that the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i) requires 'awards' or 'prizes' in the plural, consistent with the statutory requirement for extensive evidence. Not all criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) are worded in the plural—the AAO contrasted this with criteria (iv) and (ix) which use singular forms. The AAO inferred the plural has meaning, citing Maramjaya and Snapnames.com. Thus the single travel grant cannot meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of membership in Sigma Xi (redacted but identifiable from context and footnote). The submitted material indicated Sigma Xi invites to full membership 'any individual who has shown noteworthy achievement as an original investigator in a field of pure or applied science.' The petitioner noted that more than 170 members of Sigma Xi have won the Nobel Prize. Sigma Xi bylaws define noteworthy achievement as 'must be evidenced by publications, patents, written reports or a thesis or dissertation.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director did not address this criterion. The AAO found the petitioner did not submit evidence indicating what Sigma Xi considers a noteworthy achievement, and without such evidence, a noteworthy achievement is not necessarily an outstanding achievement as required by the regulation. The Nobel Prize connection was irrelevant because the petitioner was not a recipient, and the fact that Sigma Xi includes Nobel laureates does not impart distinction to the vast majority of members. The AAO also noted the petitioner provided no evidence that admittance is judged by recognized national or international experts, which is part of the plain language requirement. Additionally, the regulation requires 'memberships' in the plural, and membership in Sigma Xi alone cannot suffice. In the final merits determination, the AAO noted that Sigma Xi boasts nearly 60,000 members and does not represent only the very small percentage at the top of the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted four articles. Three were review articles that cited the petitioner's work in a footnote along with numerous articles from others in the field. The fourth was an article in a magazine called Drug Discovery and Development, which referenced an interview of the petitioner from another magazine.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined the petitioner did not meet this criterion. The AAO agreed, noting the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) requires the published material be 'about' the petitioner relating to his work. The three review articles merely cited the petitioner in footnotes—they were about recent innovations in the field, not about the petitioner. The fourth article in Drug Discovery and Development was not shown to be a professional or major trade publication or other major media, as the petitioner submitted no such supporting evidence. In the final merits determination, the AAO reiterated the articles briefly reference the petitioner or cite his work but are about the petitioner's field of endeavor, not him personally.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted seven emails from editorial staff members of scientific journals requesting his services to peer review articles, along with the petitioner's reviews of each of those articles (peer review for five journals on seven occasions). The petitioner also submitted evidence he was invited to serve as a session chair and co-chair at two scientific conferences. A supporting letter from the programming co-chair identified the session chair role at a 2009 scientific conference (AES), which included reviewing and accepting abstracts, scheduling contributions, and selecting speakers. A letter from another individual referenced the petitioner's role as session organizer at a 2006 AES conference, stating he solicited abstracts and judged the work for originality, accuracy, and relevance.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined the petitioner did not meet this criterion. The AAO disagreed in part. Regarding the 2006 conference, the AAO found the letter stated the petitioner was 'session organizer' but the petitioner's own evidence indicated he only served as co-chair, and the letter writer failed to establish how he was aware of the petitioner's role or that he was authorized to represent AES. Going on record without supporting documentation is insufficient per Matter of Soffici. Thus the 2006 conference did not qualify. However, the AAO found that the petitioner's peer review of articles for several scientific journals combined with his position as Session Chair at the 2009 AES scientific conference met the plain language requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv). In the final merits determination, the AAO noted that peer review is routine in the field and not every peer reviewer enjoys international recognition. Without evidence like reviewing for a journal with a small elite group of referees, receiving independent requests from substantial numbers of journals, or serving on an editorial board, this activity is consistent with one rising in the field but not sustained acclaim. Serving on a single occasion as Session Chair is also not indicative of sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted two patent applications (with no evidence USPTO approved them); one was licensed to a corporation for one year but the license expired with no commercial sales. Seven articles published in scientific journals (primary author on three, secondary on four), two of which received a moderate amount of citations. A chapter in a scientific handbook as primary author. Nine support letters, seven from current or former collaborators. Letters discussed work on liver cancer detection, complex codes being used by postdoctoral researchers in France, the introduction of new products at his former company, claims of Japanese commercial launch of a liver cancer detection device, certificates awarded for novel inventions (no documentation of certificates submitted), and citations from two independent researchers.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined the petitioner did not meet this criterion. The AAO agreed. Per Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp., a patent is not necessarily evidence of a track record of success; the licensed patent had no sales, the license expired, and no impact was documented. Contributions must be both original AND of major significance—the phrase 'major significance' is not superfluous (Silverman v. Eastrich). Results would need to have been reproduced, confirmed, and applied by other experts. While two articles received moderate citations, moderate citations show awareness and value but not necessarily major significance. The AAO considered citations and journal rankings (Analytical Chemistry, Electrophoresis) but found the moderate citations not persuasive of major significance. The nine letters were largely from collaborators and not wholly independent. Claims about Japanese commercial launch of the liver cancer device lacked corroborating documentation; even if accepted, use in one country doesn't show sufficient impact. Claims about Caliper developing an invention were speculative/futuristic—a petitioner must establish elements at time of filing (Matter of Katigbak). Claims about complex codes used by unnamed postdoctoral researchers in France lacked specificity. One independent citation (Mr. [redacted]) showed no reliance on petitioner's findings; another cited the petitioner among 27 citations in a single paragraph as background. Under Matter of S-A- and Matter of Y-B-, corroborative evidence is required. Under Matter of Caron International, USCIS evaluates content of expert letters. Vague, solicited letters from local colleagues are insufficient per Kazarian, 580 F.3d 1030, 1036.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of authorship of seven published scholarly articles plus one chapter in a scientific handbook related to his field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this evidence meets the plain language requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi). In the final merits determination, the AAO noted the petitioner is first author of a chapter in a 2007 scientific handbook, but four individuals who submitted support letters had authored one or more books and a total of 37 standalone chapters. Two articles garnered moderate citations; the remaining five had minimal citations. This does not support the finding that the petitioner is among the small percent who have risen to the top of the field.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner submitted qualifying evidence under only two of the three required regulatory categories (judging and scholarly articles), failing to meet the minimum eligibility requirements. Nevertheless, the AAO conducted a final merits determination and found that in the aggregate the evidence did not distinguish the petitioner as one of the small percentage at the very top of the field. The petitioner relied on a travel grant, membership in a single organization, routine peer review participation, one session chair role, two unapproved patent applications, support letters largely from collaborators, and a moderate publishing record. When compared to the accomplishments of the petitioner's own references (who had hundreds of articles, multiple awards, editorial board positions, dozens of patents, and many session chair roles), the petitioner did not measure up. Although he showed potential as a future leader, the highest level of his field was well above the level he had attained.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
Maramjaya v. USCIS, Civ. Act. No. 06-2158 (RCL) (D.C. Cir. March 26, 2008)
Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff, 2006 WL 3491005 (D. Or. Nov. 30, 2006)
Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp., 22 I&N Dec. 215 (Comm'r 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg'l Comm'r 1971)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3d Cir. 1995)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: No fraud findings. Procedural issues include: the director did not address the awards criterion or the membership criterion, requiring the AAO to conduct de novo review under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(iv); the director used a one-step analysis rather than the two-step Kazarian analysis; the petitioner failed to submit documentary corroboration for several key claims (Japanese commercial launch, certificates for novel inventions, role at 2006 conference), relying instead on assertions in support letters which the AAO found insufficient under Matter of Soffici.
